content.full_text:
January 3, 1977


Stanley R. Norton
407 Sherman Avenue
Palo Alto, CA 94306

A 99-406

Re: Statement of Economic
Interests

Dear Mr. Norton:

Michael Bennett has asked me to respond to your
letter of December 21, 1976. Thank you for confirming that
your Statement of Economic Interests filed on July 28, 1976,
is correct and complete.

You mention in your letter that you feel it is
unfair to require you as a Councilman from Palo Alto to
disclose the fee you receive from a client who resides out-
side the city. You will be pleased to know that the legis-
lature amended the Act last session to take care of just
that situation. Government Code Section 82030 of the Act,
as amended by AB 1129 effective January 1, 1977, provides
in relevant part that reportable income

does not include income received from
any source outside the jurisdiction
and not doing business within the
jurisdiction, or not having done busi-
ness within the jurisdiction during
the two years prior to the time any
statement or other action is required
under this title.

I have enclosed a copy of this bill for your review.

Sincerely yours,

Michael J. Baker
State Counsel

Enclosure

UCC: Jeanne, Mickey, Dawn

STANLEY R. NORTON
ATTORNEY AT LAW
407 SHERMAN AVENUE
PALO ALTO, CALIFORNIA 94306
TELEPHONE 324-1366

December 21, 1976


Mr. Michael Bennett
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Statement of Economic Interests

Dear Mr. Bennett:

I appreciate the thoughts contained in your letter of
October 29, 1976, a copy of which, for your convenience,
is enclosed.

To begin with, please be advised that for the report-
ing period in question (July 1, 1975-June 30, 1976), I had
no clients other than the Midpeninsula Regional Park District
(listed) from whom I received a fee of $1,000.00 or more.
Hence, I believe the Schedule 721-F, which I filed, was
correct; and as matters now stand, I do not propose to file
another or supplemental Schedule 721-F.

I do not base my position solely or primarily, as you
suggest, on the attorney-client privilege. On this point,
however, you and I both know that the opinion of a superior
court (Hays v. Wood) is generally thought to be insufficient
authority for a statewide agency to justify its legal posi-
tion.

My real concern has to do with a proper and fair inter-
pretation of the Act. It seems to me unlikely that a court
would require an attorney to list the names of clients in
situations where the receipt of a fee could not (as stated
in my statement) "reasonably or foreseeably influence a de-
cision....which could have a material financial effect on
any interests or matters referred to in Government Code Sec-
tion 87103." To put it another way, if I receive a fee of
$1,500.00 for getting Mrs. Jones, a resident of Yuba County,
a divorce, where it could have no conceivable effect on any
decision I might make as a councilman in Palo Alto, I think

it unreasonable that I should have to list the name of Mrs.
Jones on my statement. I feel very strongly about this, and
I have been wrestling with whether to, or how best to, obtain
an FPCC regulation, an attorney general's opinion, a court
decision, or whatever, to resolve the issue.

Would you be willing to support the drafting of a regu-
lation covering the Mrs. Jones' situation?

Yours sincerely,

[Signature]
STANLEY K. NORTON
Mayor, City of Palo Alto

SRN:wg

Enc:

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 29, 1976


Stanley P. Norton
Councilmember
588 No. California Ave.
Palo Alto, CA 94303

Re: Statement of Economic Interests

Dear Mr. Norton:

Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) of the Political
Reform Act requires that a public official who is an attorney
and who owns a 10 percent or greater interest in a law practice
disclose the identity of each client who pays the attorney a fee
if the attorney's pro rata share of the fee is $1,000 or more.
On the Statement of Economic Interests which you filed on July 28,
1976, you declined to disclose the identity of such clients on
the ground that the disclosure would violate the attorney-client
privilege.

Your failure to make full disclosure renders your
statement insufficient. This is particularly the case in light
of recently concluded litigation in Mendocino County Superior
Court where the validity of Section 87207(b)(2) was upheld. The
Court held in Hays v. Wood (NO. 36319, Mendocino Co. Super. Ct.,
Oct. 7, 1976) that:

Government Code Section 87207, subdivision
(b)(2) is a valid provision of law as applied
to attorney-public officials. It does not
violate the attorney-client privilege, the
right of privacy of either the attorney-
official or his clients, or the right to
equal protection of the laws. (A copy
of the court's findings and conclusions is
enclosed.)

Accordingly, the Commission has instructed the staff
to request that you file an amended Statement of Economic Interests
disclosing the names of your clients in the manner required by
the Act. Enclosed for this purpose is a supplemental Schedule
721-F which you should complete and return to this office.
Also, please remit a copy to your city clerk.


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5601 • ENFORCEMENT-CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Stanley R. Norton
October 29, 1976
Page Two


The defendant in Hays v. Wood filed a notice of appeal
on October 19, 1976. However, in view of the Superior Court's
determination, we are requesting full compliance with Section
67207(b)(2) while the appeal is pending.

In that connection, you should note that the
Commission adopted a regulation on July 20, 1976 (2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18740), which establishes a procedure whereby
public officials can seek permission from the Commission not to
disclose the name of a particular client or customer when to do
so would in fact violate a legal privilege. The court in Hays
v. Wood reaffirmed the rule that the identity of a client and
customer is not ordinarily protected by the law of privilege in
California. But in the limited circumstances where a privilege
may apply, the regulation sets forth the steps a filer should
follow to avoid disclosure (a copy of the regulation is
enclosed).

If you have any further questions concerning this
matter, please contact Michael Baker, an attorney on our staff.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:MJB:plh
Enclosures

SCHEDULE 721-F
Income of Business Entity which
Provides Legal or Brokerage Services
(Government Code Sections 82030 & 87207(b) )

See "Instruction Manual for Statement of Economic Interests — Form 721," Pages 8 and 9.

Stanley R. Norton
(Name of Business Entity)

407 Sherman Ave, Lake Alto, Calif 94306
(Address of Business Entity)

Private Law Practice
(Description of Business Activity)

List the name of each individual or entity who paid fees to the above business entity if your pro rata share of the fees was $1,000 or more:

Midpeninsula Regional Park District

(No fees were received from
other clients, in excess of
$1000, which could
reasonably or foreseeably
influence a decision
participated in by me
which reasonably or foreseeably
could have a material
financial effect on any
interests or matters referred
to in Govt. Code §c.87100.)

- 7 -
